2:
"Send thou men, that they may search the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel: of every tribe of their fathers shall ye send a man, every one a ruler among them." The witnesses note the command to send men to search the land God had given — the crux of the trial of faith at the door of promise. On "Send thou men, that they may search the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel," interpreters expound the sending. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the command — "send thou men, that they may search the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel": God bid Moses send men, one ruler from each tribe, to search the land of Canaan, the land he was giving them; the witnesses note the significance — the sending of the spies, by God's permission, in answer to the people's request (Deuteronomy 1:22, where the people first proposed it; God allowed it, though it became the occasion of their unbelief); the lesson — the trial of faith at the very door of the land (the land already given by God's promise, "which I give unto the children of Israel" — needing no spying out, for God had promised it; yet the people, not content with the promise, would search it out, and the search became the test that exposed their unbelief); the tension — God's gift sure by promise, yet the people leaning to sight rather than faith; the figure — the trial that exposes whether the heart rests on God's word; and the spying out, though permitted, was the seedbed of the unbelief that followed; the figure of faith tested at the threshold of the promise, whether the people would believe God's word or walk by sight. The figure of the trial of faith at the door of the inheritance.
